passages.explanation: Paul anticipates the objection: what advantage, then, is there in being a Jew? What value is there in circumcision? Much in every way! First of all, the Jews have been entrusted with the very words of God. What if some were unfaithful? Will their unfaithfulness nullify God's faithfulness? Not at all! Let God be true and every human being a liar. Paul addresses a series of hypothetical objections in rapid fire — a style called diatribe. If Jewish identity doesn't guarantee salvation, why bother? Because being entrusted with God's revelation is still an enormous privilege. Human unfaithfulness doesn't cancel God's faithfulness. And no, this doesn't mean we should sin more to make God's grace look better — Paul dismisses that logic with disgust.

What shall we conclude then? Do we have any advantage? Not at all! For we've already made the charge that Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin. As it is written: there is no one righteous, not even one. There is no one who understands; no one who seeks God. All have turned away, they've together become worthless. There is no one who does good, not even one. Paul strings together quotations from Psalms, Ecclesiastes, and Isaiah to build an airtight case. The verdict: the whole world is accountable to God. No one — Jew or Gentile — can claim innocence. The law's function isn't to save but to make people conscious of sin. This is the lowest point of the argument — total human failure across the board. Everyone needs rescue.

But now apart from the law the righteousness of God has been made known, to which the Law and the Prophets testify. This righteousness is given through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe. There is no difference between Jew and Gentile, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and all are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus. God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement, through the shedding of his blood — to be received by faith. He did this to demonstrate his righteousness, because in his forbearance he had left the sins committed beforehand unpunished. He did it to demonstrate his righteousness at the present time, so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus. This is the heart of Romans and arguably the most important paragraph Paul ever wrote. The solution to universal sin is universal grace. Righteousness comes through faith, not law-keeping. God is both just (sin is dealt with through Christ's death) and the justifier (he declares sinners righteous through faith). The cross solves God's dilemma: how to forgive without being unjust.

Where, then, is boasting? It is excluded. Because of what law? The law that requires works? No, because of the law that requires faith. For we maintain that a person is justified by faith apart from the works of the law. Or is God the God of Jews only? Isn't he the God of Gentiles too? Yes, of Gentiles too, since there is only one God, who will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith. Do we, then, nullify the law by this faith? Not at all! Rather, we uphold the law. If salvation is by faith, no one can boast about earning it. And if there's one God for everyone, the method of salvation must be the same for everyone. Faith doesn't destroy the law — it fulfils what the law was always pointing toward.
